user_prompt: Explain Bernoulli’s principle through an airplane wing example in depth

style_bible.visual_style: Clean, high-contrast 3D animation focusing on a detailed airplane wing model with airflow visualization using smooth blue and red gradient streams.
style_bible.camera_rules: Use a mix of slow dolly and track shots to follow airflow; hold shots to emphasize text overlays; consistent bottom-left placement for text; no abrupt cuts, smooth transitions.
style_bible.lighting_rules: Soft, even daylight simulation with subtle directional key light from top-left to highlight wing contours; no harsh shadows; consistent lighting across scenes.
style_bible.continuity_rules: Primary subject (airplane wing) remains centered and consistent in scale and orientation; airflow visualization colors and style remain unchanged; camera direction always from front-left side of wing; lighting and color palette constant; scenes flow logically from wing overview to airflow mechanism to pressure effect.

scenes.prompt:
SCENE INTENT: Introduce the airplane wing and the concept of airflow split.

GLOBAL CONTINUITY:
- primary subject: airplane wing with airflow visualization
- environment: clear sky background with subtle gradient
- constants: camera front-left, soft daylight, blue/red airflow colors
- explanatory state: Scene 1 setup

NARRATION BEAT MAP:
- 0–3s: "Here is an airplane wing slicing through the air."
- 3–7s: "Air splits above and below the wing as it moves forward."
- 7–10s: "Notice the airflow paths and their differences."

ON-SCREEN TEXT OVERLAY PLAN:
- 1.0–3.0s: "Airplane Wing" (position: bottom-left, large, clean sans-serif, fade in/out)
- 3.2–6.5s: "Air Splits" (same style/position)
- 7.2–9.5s: "Flow Paths" (same style/position)

SHOT 1 (0–3s):
- framing: Wide medium shot showing entire wing from front-left angle
- lens: 35mm
- camera movement: slow dolly forward
- subject & action: by 1s, wing appears centered with airflow lines starting; by 2s, airflow splits clearly above and below wing; by 3s, airflow paths are distinct with blue above and red below
- continuity note: airflow split and wing orientation set for next scene

SHOT 2 (3–7s):
- framing: Closer side view focusing on upper and lower wing surfaces
- lens: 50mm
- camera movement: hold steady
- subject & action: airflow lines remain visible; by 5s, subtle animation shows faster flow above wing; by 7s, flow speed difference is clear
- continuity note: airflow speed difference emphasized, preparing for pressure explanation

SHOT 3 (7–10s):
- framing: Tight close-up on wing cross-section with airflow lines
- lens: 85mm
- camera movement: slow pan from leading edge to trailing edge
- subject & action: by 8s, airflow lines highlight curvature; by 10s, final frame shows clear flow paths and wing shape
- continuity note: final frame with clear airflow paths and wing curvature for handoff
scenes.negative_prompt: watermark, logo, brand mark, random unreadable text, gibberish text, misspelled text, extra limbs, distorted faces, flicker, glitch, blurry, lowres, people, human, face, hands, cockpit
scenes.camera: Start with slow dolly forward (0–3s), hold steady (3–7s), slow pan right (7–10s)
scenes.audio_cue: 0–3s: Here is an airplane wing slicing through the air. 3–7s: Air splits above and below the wing as it moves forward. 7–10s: Notice the airflow paths and their differences.

scenes.prompt:
SCENE INTENT: Explain Bernoulli’s principle by showing airflow speed differences and pressure effects.

GLOBAL CONTINUITY:
- primary subject: airplane wing with airflow visualization
- environment: clear sky background with subtle gradient
- constants: camera front-left, soft daylight, blue/red airflow colors
- explanatory state: Scene 2 mechanism

NARRATION BEAT MAP:
- 0–3s: "Faster airflow above the wing lowers pressure."
- 3–7s: "Slower airflow below means higher pressure."
- 7–10s: "This pressure difference creates lift."

ON-SCREEN TEXT OVERLAY PLAN:
- 1.0–3.0s: "Faster Air Above" (position: bottom-left, large, clean sans-serif, fade in/out)
- 3.2–6.5s: "Higher Pressure Below" (same style/position)
- 7.2–9.5s: "Lift Force" (same style/position)

SHOT 1 (0–3s):
- framing: Close-up on upper wing surface airflow lines
- lens: 85mm
- camera movement: hold steady
- subject & action: by 1s, blue airflow lines speed up and become thinner; by 3s, animated speed lines emphasize velocity increase
- continuity note: airflow speed visualization continues for pressure comparison

SHOT 2 (3–7s):
- framing: Close-up on lower wing surface airflow lines
- lens: 85mm
- camera movement: hold steady
- subject & action: by 4s, red airflow lines slow and thicken; by 7s, pressure difference arrows appear showing higher pressure below
- continuity note: pressure difference arrows remain for lift explanation

SHOT 3 (7–10s):
- framing: Medium shot showing wing with pressure arrows and upward lift vector
- lens: 50mm
- camera movement: slow dolly out to reveal full wing and lift arrow
- subject & action: by 8s, upward lift arrow grows; by 10s, final frame shows wing, airflow, pressure arrows, and lift vector clearly
- continuity note: final frame sets up for consequence of lift in next scene
scenes.negative_prompt: watermark, logo, brand mark, random unreadable text, gibberish text, misspelled text, extra limbs, distorted faces, flicker, glitch, blurry, lowres, people, human, face, hands, cockpit
scenes.camera: Hold steady close-ups (0–7s), slow dolly out (7–10s)
scenes.audio_cue: 0–3s: Faster airflow above the wing lowers pressure. 3–7s: Slower airflow below means higher pressure. 7–10s: This pressure difference creates lift.

scenes.prompt:
SCENE INTENT: Show the consequence of Bernoulli’s principle: lift enabling flight.

GLOBAL CONTINUITY:
- primary subject: airplane wing with airflow, pressure, and lift vectors
- environment: clear sky background with subtle gradient
- constants: camera front-left, soft daylight, blue/red airflow colors
- explanatory state: Scene 3 consequence

NARRATION BEAT MAP:
- 0–3s: "Lift pushes the wing upward against gravity."
- 3–7s: "This force allows airplanes to fly."
- 7–10s: "Bernoulli’s principle is key to flight."

ON-SCREEN TEXT OVERLAY PLAN:
- 1.0–3.0s: "Lift Pushes Up" (position: bottom-left, large, clean sans-serif, fade in/out)
- 3.2–6.5s: "Airplane Flies" (same style/position)
- 7.2–9.5s: "Bernoulli’s Principle" (same style/position)

SHOT 1 (0–3s):
- framing: Medium shot of wing with large upward lift arrow and subtle gravity arrow down
- lens: 50mm
- camera movement: slow track upward following lift arrow
- subject & action: by 2s, lift arrow pulses gently; gravity arrow steady
- continuity note: lift and gravity vectors emphasized

SHOT 2 (3–7s):
- framing: Wide shot showing wing ascending with airflow and lift
- lens: 35mm
- camera movement: slow dolly back and slight pan up
- subject & action: by 5s, wing moves upward smoothly; airflow lines remain
- continuity note: wing ascent sets up final explanatory frame

SHOT 3 (7–10s):
- framing: Stable wide shot of wing flying with all annotations visible
- lens: 35mm
- camera movement: hold steady
- subject & action: final frame shows wing, airflow, pressure, and lift text overlays
- continuity note: final stable frame concludes explanation

FINAL FRAME HANDOFF: Wing flying upward with visible airflow lines, pressure arrows below and above, and a prominent upward lift vector; text overlay "Bernoulli’s Principle" bottom-left, stable frame for video end or further content.
scenes.negative_prompt: watermark, logo, brand mark, random unreadable text, gibberish text, misspelled text, extra limbs, distorted faces, flicker, glitch, blurry, lowres, people, human, face, hands, cockpit
scenes.camera: Slow track upward (0–3s), slow dolly back with pan up (3–7s), hold steady (7–10s)
scenes.audio_cue: 0–3s: Lift pushes the wing upward against gravity. 3–7s: This force allows airplanes to fly. 7–10s: Bernoulli’s principle is key to flight.
